skill.name: haskell-pro
skill.description: Expert Haskell engineer specializing in advanced type systems, pure functional design, and high-reliability software. Use PROACTIVELY for type-level programming, concurrency, and architecture guidance.
skill.summary: Guides advanced Haskell design, type-safe architecture, concurrency, performance, parsing, and testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported one external command pattern and six weak cryptography patterns, but review found no executable code or cryptographic implementation. The external command hit is Markdown inline code around a resource path, and the weak crypto hits are Haskell domain terms such as type systems, pure functions, and typeclasses. No prompt injection, exfiltration, network access, environment access, or filesystem mutation was found in the reviewed skill file.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: SKILL.md line 25 uses Markdown inline code around a resource path and asks the assistant to open documentation. It does not execute Ruby, shell, or any external command, so the reported backtick execution is a false positive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a Markdown-formatted path only. There is no command interpreter, script block, shell syntax, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: SKILL.md lines 3-4, 27, 31, 33, and 42 contain Haskell domain language about type systems, pure functions, and typeclasses. No hash, cipher, crypto API, key handling, or security-sensitive algorithm appears in those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are natural-language Haskell guidance, not source code. The surrounding context confirms the terms describe programming concepts rather than cryptographic algorithms.

content.value_statement: Haskell projects often need careful type design, isolated effects, and practical performance choices. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code provide focused Haskell architecture, implementation, and review guidance.
actual_capabilities: Designs Haskell domain models using newtypes, phantom types, GADTs, and type families when useful.
Separates pure logic from IO boundaries in functional architectures.
Reviews concurrency designs using STM, async, lightweight threads, and exception-safe combinators.
Suggests performance improvements involving strictness, profiling, and fusion.
Improves Cabal or Stack project structure, module organization, and dependency hygiene.
Provides testing guidance with QuickCheck, Hspec, and property-based reasoning.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, but it does not run GHC, Cabal, Stack, or tests by itself.
The referenced implementation playbook is not included in the generated file structure.
It is focused on Haskell and is not suitable for unrelated languages or tools.
It should not replace security review for cryptography, authentication, or production secrets.

use_cases.title: Model a domain safely
use_cases.description: Create Haskell types that encode invariants and reduce invalid states before runtime.

use_cases.title: Review service architecture
use_cases.description: Check module boundaries, effect handling, parsing, and IO isolation in a Haskell service.

use_cases.title: Improve reliability tests
use_cases.description: Plan QuickCheck and Hspec coverage for business rules, parsers, and concurrency behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Haskell concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain this Haskell concept in practical terms. Include a small example, common mistakes, and when to use it: [concept].

prompt_templates.title: Design typed domain models
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me model this domain in Haskell with strong types. Identify invariants, propose newtypes or algebraic data types, and show the module shape: [domain details].

prompt_templates.title: Review Haskell architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Haskell design for type safety, IO boundaries, module structure, dependency hygiene, and testability. Prioritize issues and concrete fixes: [design or code summary].

prompt_templates.title: Optimize concurrent Haskell
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Haskell concurrency or performance problem. Consider STM, async, strictness, profiling, fusion, exception safety, and measurable verification steps: [problem details].

output_examples.input: Design a safer representation for user registration data.
output_examples.output: The response would identify invalid states, propose named types for validated email and password values, separate parsing from business logic, and list property tests.

output_examples.input: Review a Haskell worker that uses async tasks and shared state.
output_examples.output: The response would examine exception propagation, cancellation behavior, STM boundaries, resource cleanup, and tests for failure paths.

output_examples.input: Improve a Cabal project that has unclear modules and slow builds.
output_examples.output: The response would suggest module grouping, dependency cleanup, profiling steps, strictness checks, and a small verification plan.

best_practices: Provide the relevant type signatures, module names, and build tool before asking for implementation advice.
Ask for invariants, error cases, and test properties before requesting final code.
Verify suggested examples with GHCi, Cabal, Stack, and your project test suite.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill for non-Haskell implementation work.
Do not ask for advanced abstractions without explaining the maintenance problem they should solve.
Do not accept performance advice without profiling data or a clear benchmark target.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps with Haskell type design, pure functional architecture, concurrency, parsing, performance, project structure, and tests.

faq.question: Can it write complete Haskell modules?
faq.answer: Yes, it can draft module designs and examples, but you should compile and test them in your environment.

faq.question: Does it require Cabal or Stack?
faq.answer: No. It can discuss either build tool and can adapt to the project details you provide.

faq.question: Can it help beginners?
faq.answer: Yes. It can explain concepts and examples, but the skill is strongest when used for practical Haskell design decisions.

faq.question: Does it perform security analysis?
faq.answer: It can discuss safer Haskell patterns, but it is not a dedicated security audit skill.

faq.question: What should I include in my prompt?
faq.answer: Include the goal, constraints, relevant types, error cases, build tool, and any failing tests or compiler errors.
